title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who discuss their experience of loneliness and how they overcame it. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Finding Each Other: Four People on Loneliness and Community

texts.A:
### Marianne, 58, Administrator
When both my children left home within a year of each other, I was not prepared for how empty the house would feel. [43]I had always defined myself primarily as a mother, and without that daily role to structure my time and my identity, I experienced a loss that I think is rarely talked about honestly enough.[/43] I went through about a year of quiet but acute loneliness before I did anything practical about it. [47]A friend persuaded me to come to a local book club, which I attended with zero enthusiasm and immediately loved – the combination of intellectual discussion and genuine warmth was exactly what I hadn't known I was missing.[/47] [51]I now run the book club myself, and when I watch new members arrive looking slightly nervous and leave looking lighter, I recognise exactly what is happening and it means a great deal to me.[/51]
texts.B:
### Kofi, 25, Postgraduate Student
[44]I moved from Ghana to Edinburgh for my postgraduate degree and the cultural adjustment was far greater than anything the university's welcome materials had prepared me for.[/44] The social dynamics were different, the weather was relentless, and I found British social reserve genuinely difficult to navigate at first. [48]Joining the university's African and Caribbean Society was transformative – not because I only wanted to socialise within my own community, but because it gave me a secure base from which to engage more confidently with wider university life.[/48] [52]What surprised me was how warmly my Scottish classmates eventually opened up once they trusted I was staying – the initial coolness was reserve rather than hostility, and understanding that distinction made a real difference to how I felt.[/52]
texts.C:
### Elspeth, 71, Retired Librarian
[45]I had worked in the same library for thirty-four years, and when I retired I lost not only my sense of purpose but my entire social structure in a single day.[/45] My colleagues had been my community, my routine had shaped my week, and suddenly both were gone. [49]What helped most was volunteering at a community garden, where the work is physical, outdoors and collaborative – it filled the hours in a way that television or housework simply couldn't.[/49] I go four mornings a week now, and the people I've met there are among the most interesting I've encountered in a long life. I'd recommend volunteering to anyone navigating the transition out of full-time work.
texts.D:
### Nathan, 35, Freelance Illustrator
[46]I've worked from home for eight years and the isolation crept up on me so gradually that I didn't recognise it as loneliness until I realised I was going entire weeks without a meaningful conversation with another adult.[/46] The flexibility of freelancing is wonderful, but the lack of structure and the absence of casual human contact are real costs that anyone considering it should take seriously. [50]I rented a desk in a shared co-working space, and it turned out to be the single most effective change I made – even on days when I say almost nothing to anyone, simply being around other working people fundamentally changes how I feel.[/50] I've since also joined a local life-drawing class, which has been unexpectedly sociable for a group ostensibly focused on staring at a model.

questions.question: felt a sense of lost identity, not just lost company, when their circumstances changed?
questions.explanation: Marianne says she had always defined herself primarily as a mother, and losing that daily role meant losing a sense of who she was, not just who she spent time with.

questions.question: encountered cultural barriers when they moved to a new country that made forming connections more difficult?
questions.explanation: Kofi moved from Ghana to Edinburgh and found that different social dynamics and British reserve made the cultural adjustment far greater than expected.

questions.question: experienced their loneliness as a direct consequence of leaving long-term employment?
questions.explanation: Elspeth says she lost not only purpose but her entire social structure the day she retired from thirty-four years in the same library.

questions.question: found that the isolation of their working situation had built up slowly without them initially noticing?
questions.explanation: Nathan says isolation crept up so gradually that he didn't recognise it as loneliness until he realised he was going weeks without a meaningful conversation.

questions.question: was encouraged to try an activity by another person and found it unexpectedly fulfilling?
questions.explanation: Marianne was persuaded by a friend to attend a book club she went to with zero enthusiasm and immediately loved.

questions.question: found that joining a group connected to their own background gave them the confidence to engage more broadly?
questions.explanation: Kofi says joining the African and Caribbean Society gave him a secure base from which to engage more confidently with wider university life.

questions.question: found that physical, outdoor, collaborative activity was the most effective remedy for their sense of isolation?
questions.explanation: Elspeth says volunteering at a community garden – physical, outdoors and collaborative – filled the hours in a way television or housework couldn't.

questions.question: describes a practical change to their working environment that significantly improved their sense of connection?
questions.explanation: Nathan rented a desk in a co-working space and describes it as the single most effective change he made, as simply being around other people changes how he feels.

questions.question: now actively helps others who appear to be experiencing the same kind of loneliness they once felt?
questions.explanation: Marianne now runs the book club and says recognising new members' nervousness and relief means a great deal to her.

questions.question: was reassured to discover that what they had interpreted as unfriendliness towards them had a different explanation?
questions.explanation: Kofi says understanding that his Scottish classmates' initial coolness was reserve rather than hostility made a real difference to how he felt.
